title: Pack Your Trunks and Move to Poole | Wolves
meta: A friendly relocation guide to moving from Sussex to Poole, Dorset: the journey, neighbourhoods and planning a smooth long-distance move.
h1: Pack Your Trunks and Move to Poole: A Sussex-to-Dorset Relocation Guide
lead: Poole has a way of turning a day-trip into a daydream. The huge natural harbour, the long sweep of Sandbanks, the bustling quay and the easy reach of the Purbeck hills all add up to one of the most appealing places to live on the south coast. If a move from Sussex to Dorset has gone from idle thought to firm plan, this guide will help you pack your trunks and move to Poole with your sanity intact, covering the journey itself, the neighbourhoods worth knowing, what a long-distance move actually involves, and how to make the whole thing feel less like an ordeal and more like the fresh start it should be.
body:
Why so many people are choosing to move to Poole

Poole sits on the Dorset coast around 110 miles west of our base near Pulborough, and the draw is easy to understand. It boasts one of the largest natural harbours in the world, a genuinely lovely stretch of Blue Flag beaches running through to Bournemouth, and a town centre that has managed to keep its independent shops and waterside character. For anyone leaving the South East in search of a slower pace, more outdoor living and a stronger sense of seaside community, it ticks an awful lot of boxes.

It is also a practical choice. Poole has a working ferry port with sailings to the Channel Islands and France, mainline rail connections up to London Waterloo, and the wider Bournemouth conurbation on its doorstep for jobs, hospitals, universities and shopping. You get the feeling of a coastal town without the isolation that sometimes comes with one. That balance of lifestyle and convenience is exactly what tempts so many people to make the leap.

The journey from Sussex to Dorset

A long-distance move is a different beast from a local one, and the journey is the part people most often underestimate. From West Sussex, the route to Poole runs west along the A27 and M27 past Southampton, then onto the A31 through the New Forest and down into Dorset. On a good day it is a comfortable two to two-and-a-half hours by car, but a fully loaded removal lorry travelling at a steadier pace, and very possibly hitting traffic around Southampton or the New Forest in summer, should be allowed considerably longer.

This matters for planning. A cross-county move is rarely a quick dash there and back, so the timing of loading, travelling and unloading needs thinking through carefully, particularly if completion on your new home falls on the same day as your sale. We handle long-distance relocations regularly as part of our house removals service, and the single biggest favour you can do yourself is to build in realistic time rather than assuming everything will run to the minute. A move that crosses several counties leaves less room for the day to absorb surprises.

Getting to know Poole's neighbourhoods

Poole is bigger and more varied than first-time visitors expect, and where you settle will shape your daily life enormously. Spending a little time understanding the areas before you commit is time very well spent.

Sandbanks and the harbour edge

Sandbanks is the name everyone knows, a slender peninsula of beach and high-value homes guarding the harbour mouth. It is famous, beautiful and expensive, with some of the most sought-after addresses on the south coast. Living here means waking up to water on both sides and a beach on your doorstep, though it comes at a premium and the summer crowds are very real.

Lower Parkstone and Ashley Cross

For something with more of a village feel, the Lower Parkstone and Ashley Cross area is a favourite. It has independent cafes, delis and pubs clustered around Ashley Cross green, leafy streets of period homes, and an easy walk down to the harbour. It tends to appeal to families and professionals who want character and community without being right on the tourist trail.

Broadstone and Canford Heath

Further inland, Broadstone offers a more traditional suburban setting with good schools, green space and a strong local high street, making it popular with families. Canford Heath sits beside a large nature reserve, with plenty of walking and a quieter, greener atmosphere. Both trade a little of the seaside immediacy for more house for your money and a calmer pace.

Poole Old Town and the Quay

If you want to be in the thick of it, the Old Town and Quay area puts you among cobbled streets, historic buildings, restaurants and the working harbour. It suits those who love being able to stroll to the water, eat out and feel the buzz of the town, and who do not mind a livelier setting in the warmer months.

What a long-distance move actually involves

Moving across the country is not simply a bigger version of moving down the road; it changes the logistics in ways worth understanding before you book anything.

One trip, not several

On a local move, a removal team can shuttle back and forth if needed. Over 100-plus miles, that is neither practical nor affordable, so everything you own needs to fit on the vehicle in a single, well-organised load. That puts a premium on accurate planning: the right size of lorry, sensible loading order, and a clear sense of volume so nothing is left stranded. A pre-move survey, which we offer as standard, takes the guesswork out of this and lets us turn up with the right vehicle and crew.

Timing and the dreaded chain

Long-distance moves and property chains are an uneasy pairing. If your sale and purchase complete on the same day, your belongings may be on the road while solicitors release funds, and any delay leaves a lorry-load of furniture with nowhere to go. This is exactly where a contingency plan earns its keep, and it is the reason so many cross-county movers build storage into their arrangements. More on that shortly.

Packing for the long haul

Items endure more handling and more miles on a long-distance move, so packing properly is not optional. Boxes need to be full, firm and squarely stackable; fragile items need real protection; and furniture benefits from being wrapped and, where possible, disassembled. If the thought of packing an entire house fills you with dread, our full packing service takes the whole job off your hands, and there is plenty of practical advice in our moving and storage tips if you would rather tackle it yourself.

Using storage to take the pressure off

One of the most useful tools for a long-distance move is storage, and it solves the two problems that cause the most stress: timing gaps and the sheer logistics of distance.

If your move-out and move-in dates do not line up, or the chain wobbles at the last minute, having your belongings in a secure unit means a hiccup does not become a disaster. Equally, many people moving to a new part of the country prefer to move into a rental first, get to know the area, and only then buy, in which case storing the bulk of their possessions while they settle makes complete sense. Our short-term storage is built for exactly these in-between periods, while long-term storage suits anyone who needs a longer, better-value arrangement. You can browse the full range on our storage solutions page, and if you are unsure how much room you will need, the storage size calculator gives you a quick, no-pressure estimate in a few minutes.

Storage also smooths the practical side of relocating. Rather than racing to empty a lorry into a half-sorted new home in a single exhausting day, you can hold back the things you will not need immediately, settle in at a sensible pace, and bring the rest across when you are ready. It is a gentler way to move a long way from home.

Getting Poole right room by room

When you do reach moving day, a little thought about how the lorry is loaded and unloaded saves hours at the far end. The golden rule on a long-distance move is that the order things come off the vehicle should match the order you want to deal with them. Boxes for the kitchen and the children's bedrooms tend to be wanted first, so they should come off early, while garage and loft items can wait until last. Labelling every box clearly with its destination room, and marking the fragile ones, means our crew can place each one where it belongs rather than stacking everything in the hallway for you to redistribute later.

It also helps to plan the new home's layout before you arrive. A rough floor plan showing where the large furniture goes lets the crew set up beds, sofas and wardrobes straight away, so that by the end of an exhausting travel day you have somewhere comfortable to sit and sleep rather than a maze of furniture to wrestle with. These small pieces of forethought make an enormous difference when you have just travelled the length of two counties.

Don't forget the essentials box

On any move, but especially a long one, pack an essentials box that travels with you in the car rather than on the lorry: kettle, mugs, tea, a few snacks, toiletries, phone chargers, a change of clothes and any medication. Arriving in Poole tired and hungry with no idea which box holds the kettle is a small misery easily avoided. Keep important documents and valuables with you too, so nothing vital is ever out of your sight during the journey.

Settling into life in Poole

Once the boxes are in, the real adventure begins. Poole rewards exploration: spend a weekend walking the harbour, take the chain ferry across to Studland and the Purbeck coast, and get your bearings on the high street and the quay. Sorting out the practical essentials early, registering with a GP, finding the nearest recycling centre, working out the school run if you have children, helps the new place start to feel like home faster.

The outdoor lifestyle is one of Poole's great gifts. Sailing, paddleboarding and open-water swimming are all on your doorstep, the New Forest is a short drive north for walking and cycling, and the Jurassic Coast stretches west for some of the finest coastal scenery in England. After the South East, the sense of space and the closeness of the sea can feel like a genuine change of life rather than just a change of postcode.

Give yourself permission to take the settling-in slowly. It is tempting to try to unpack everything in the first weekend, but a long-distance move is tiring and there is no prize for finishing fastest. Prioritise the rooms you use daily, then work through the rest over the following weeks as you discover how you actually want to live in the new space. Many people find their preferences shift once they are in a new home and a new town, so leaving some boxes for a little while, ideally the non-essential ones you held back in storage, can save you arranging things twice. Use the early weeks to explore your new neighbourhood on foot, find your favourite coffee spot and chat to the neighbours; those small connections are what turn a new address into a home far faster than a perfectly unpacked house ever could.

Planning your move from Sussex with confidence

A relocation of this scale benefits enormously from early planning, and a clear timeline removes most of the anxiety. As a rough guide, start gathering quotes and booking your remover six to eight weeks ahead, begin decluttering and sorting paperwork at the same time, and tackle non-essential packing two to three weeks before the day. Our guide to planning a house move walks through a sensible week-by-week countdown, and if you are also juggling a temporary rental or a gap between homes, our piece on living in between homes is well worth a read.

Decluttering before a long-distance move is especially worthwhile, because you pay, in time and money, to transport everything you own across the country. Moving is the perfect moment to be honest about what you actually use and love, and our advice on downsizing your home can help you decide what makes the journey and what does not.

Why move with Wolves Removals

We are a family-run removals and storage company based near Pulborough in West Sussex, and we have been helping people move since 2016, including plenty of long-distance relocations across the country. We cover West and East Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Kent, with nationwide and European moves too, so a move from Sussex to Dorset is firmly within our day-to-day work.

As a LAPADA member and a Checkatrade-verified business, we hold ourselves to proper professional standards, and we are fully insured with liability cover up to £10 million, so your belongings are protected on every mile of the journey. We pride ourselves on honest, transparent pricing with no hidden extras, friendly crews who treat your possessions as their own, and the kind of careful planning a long-distance move demands.

If Poole is calling, we would love to help you get there. You can get a sense of our costs on the pricing page, request a tailored figure through our free quote service, or simply call our friendly team on 01903 893731 to talk it through. Pack your trunks; Dorset is waiting.
faqs: How far is Poole from Sussex and how long does the move take?

Poole is roughly 110 miles west of our Pulborough base, around two to two-and-a-half hours by car via the M27 and A31. A loaded removal lorry travels at a steadier pace and may meet traffic around Southampton or the New Forest, so we always build in generous time for the journey and plan the day around a single, well-organised trip rather than a quick dash there and back.
Do you cover long-distance moves from Sussex to Dorset?

Yes. We handle long-distance relocations regularly as part of our house removals service. We cover West and East Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Kent, with nationwide and European moves too, so a Sussex-to-Poole move is well within our normal work. We will carry out a survey, bring the right size vehicle and crew, and plan the timing carefully.
Should I use storage when moving long-distance to Poole?

Storage is one of the most useful tools for a long move. It covers any gap between your move-out and move-in dates, gives you a safety net if a property chain wobbles, and lets many people move into a rental first to get to know the area before buying. Our short-term storage suits in-between periods, and long-term storage works better for longer arrangements.
How early should I book a long-distance move?

We recommend gathering quotes and booking your remover six to eight weeks ahead for a long-distance move, as the right vehicle and crew need to be reserved for a full day. Starting to declutter and pack non-essentials a few weeks before the date keeps things calm. Our planning-for-a-house-move guide sets out a sensible week-by-week countdown.
Can you pack everything for me as well as move it?

We can. Our full packing service takes the entire job off your hands, which is especially worthwhile on a long move where belongings face more handling and more miles. Proper packing, full and firmly stacked boxes, well-protected fragile items and wrapped furniture, keeps everything safe over the distance. Just let us know what you need when you request a quote.
